question.text: An investor holds 100 fully paid-up equity shares and 50 partly paid-up equity shares of the same company in demat form. Are these shares fungible with each other?

answer.text: No, because fungibility applies only to securities in the same class, and fully paid-up and partly paid-up are different classes
Yes, because they are shares of the same company
Yes, because all demat shares are fungible regardless of paid-up status
No, because individual shares can be identified by distinctive numbers in demat form

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Fungibility means all securities in the same class are identical and interchangeable. Fully paid-up shares and partly paid-up shares are different classes, so they are not fungible with each other. **The Trap:** 'All demat shares are fungible regardless' is wrong — fungibility is limited to the same class. Even in demat form, different classes (fully paid, partly paid, different series) remain distinct. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII discusses fungibility in the context of derivative contracts where contract specifications define the 'class'. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The 100 fully paid shares are fungible among themselves (any one is interchangeable with any other), but not with the 50 partly paid shares, which form a separate class.
